Executive Summary

Executive Summary

This is a 5-day-out planning flight — forecast maturity is limited and no TAFs, winds aloft, icing charts, or turbulence data are yet available for the flight date. Based on extended prog charts valid near March 30, the Pacific Northwest pattern shows a developing low off the Pacific coast with troughing pushing into the West, which puts San Carlos to Bend potentially in the path of incoming Pacific moisture and frontal activity. Primary Risk: An incoming Pacific trough/front may bring IMC, icing, and mountain-wave turbulence over the Oregon Cascades on or around your departure day. This is a MARGINAL GO — provisional, pending significant forecast updates in the 24–48 hours before the flight. The single most important variable to watch is the position and timing of the approaching Pacific low and associated frontal system relative to the KSQL–KBDN corridor on March 30.

Operational Briefing

The Day Before — What to Watch

Sunday evening, March 29, run a full weather check. At 5 days out right now, the extended progs show a trough/low moving into the Pacific Northwest — but the timing and intensity are highly uncertain. By Sunday evening you'll have TAFs, winds aloft forecasts, and likely G-AIRMETs in hand. That's when this flight becomes a real go/no-go decision.

Sunday night checklist:

  • Check the KSQL TAF — specifically for ceilings and visibility at 14:00Z departure. Any BKN/OVC below 3,000 ft or visibility under 3 SM is a yellow flag.
  • Check the KBDN TAF — Bend sits east of the Cascades in the high desert. Arrival conditions can diverge sharply from coastal weather, but frontal passage can bring rapid ceiling drops and gusty winds. Watch for ceilings below 3,000 AGL and wind gusts exceeding 25 kt.
  • Pull the SLC and PDX AFDs — forecasters will discuss the trough timing and Cascade impacts explicitly.
  • Check icing charts (CIP/FIP) for the 6,000–14,000 ft band over the Oregon Cascades — that's your critical icing window on this route.
  • Check G-AIRMETs for IFR, TURB, and ICE along the entire KSQL–KBDN corridor.
  • Check mountain wave SIGMETs — if the trough is pushing strong westerlies over the Cascades, mountain wave is possible and could be significant at 12,000 ft.

If the trough has already moved through and post-frontal high pressure is building over Oregon by Sunday evening, this flight likely becomes a clean go. If the front is still approaching or sitting on the route, you're in marginal to no-go territory.

Leaving KSQL — Departure at 2026-03-30 14:00Z

TAF status: Not yet valid. No ceiling/visibility data available for this time window.

San Carlos sits in the Bay Area, which has its own microclimate complexities. At 14:00Z (6 AM local), marine layer is the standard concern. The Bay Area marine layer in late March can produce low ceilings and reduced visibility on coastal and near-coastal fields. However, whether that's a factor on March 30 depends entirely on whether a Pacific frontal system is sitting on the coast or has already passed.

Based on the extended prog trajectory (Day 5–7 charts), a low is developing off the Pacific Northwest coast. If that system is still approaching on the 30th, expect the possibility of low ceilings, reduced visibility, and potential light rain at KSQL. If it's already passed, the Bay Area could be in the clear post-frontal with good VFR.

Bottom line on departure: Cannot assess with confidence at this range. Check Sunday night. If KSQL is VFR with reasonable ceilings and visibility at 14:00Z, departure is manageable. The climb-out over the Bay Area and northward toward the Sacramento Valley should be straightforward in post-frontal conditions — but could involve layer penetration if the front hasn't cleared.

The Ride at 12,000 ft

No winds aloft, FIP icing charts, turbulence charts (GTG), or G-AIRMETs are available at this 5-day range. All hazard assessments below are CONDITIONAL and LOW CONFIDENCE based on synoptic pattern alone.

Icing

This route crosses the northern California coastal ranges, the Sacramento Valley, and then the Oregon Cascades before descending to Bend on the east side. The Cascades at 12,000 ft are your primary icing concern on any day with Pacific moisture present.

Altitude Icing Risk (Conditional) Notes
6,000–9,000 ft CONDITIONAL / LOW CONFIDENCE Possible mixed icing in cloud layers if frontal moisture present; below freezing level likely in late March
9,000–12,000 ft CONDITIONAL / LOW CONFIDENCE Most exposed altitude over the Cascades if moisture is present; classic rime icing environment
Above 14,000 ft LOW Likely above significant cloud tops in post-frontal air; colder and drier but no FIP data to confirm

SLD Risk: No SLD signal indicated — but no FIP data exists at this range. Cannot assess. If FIP charts on Sunday show SLD probability over the Cascades, that's a hard stop for most GA aircraft.

Moisture Gating: Icing requires moisture. If the front has cleared and post-frontal dry air is in place by the 30th, icing risk drops significantly. If the front is still in place, icing over the Cascades between 8,000–13,000 ft is a genuine concern.

Turbulence and Ride Quality

Why this happens: The Oregon Cascades are a significant north-south barrier. When westerly or southwesterly flow is strong aloft ahead of or behind a Pacific trough, wind-over-terrain generates mountain wave, rotor zones, and mechanical turbulence — particularly on the lee (east) side where Bend sits.

At 12,000 ft, you're right in the prime mountain wave turbulence zone over the Cascades. Without winds aloft data, I can't give you a specific call, but the synoptic pattern suggests:

  • If winds at 12,000 ft over the Cascades are 30–40 kt from the west/southwest (common ahead of Pacific troughs), expect light to moderate turbulence — LOCALIZED over the ridge crossings.
  • If winds exceed 40 kt, moderate turbulence is possible with mountain wave. CONDITIONAL on actual winds aloft.
  • Post-frontal high pressure building would mean calmer winds, smoother ride.

Altitude Strategy

  • Best altitude band: Above 14,000 ft (if certified/equipped) to get above cloud tops and the worst wave turbulence; OR below 10,000 ft in the Sacramento Valley segment before any Cascade crossing. At 12,000 ft you're in the worst of both worlds over the Cascades — middle of the icing layer and prime wave turbulence altitude.
  • Worst altitude band: 9,000–12,000 ft over the Cascades — in cloud, in the icing zone, in the wave turbulence band simultaneously if frontal moisture is present.
  • Escape option: Descend to below 8,000 ft on the east side if icing is encountered — Bend sits at 3,460 ft MSL so terrain clears quickly east of the Cascades. Alternatively, if equipped for higher altitudes and above the icing/cloud tops, climbing above 14,000 ft provides the cleanest air.

Other Hazards

Hazard Risk Level Leg What to Expect
Icing over Cascades CONDITIONAL Northern CA / Oregon Cascades Depends on frontal position; check FIP Sunday
Mountain Wave / Turbulence CONDITIONAL Cascade crossing Light to moderate if westerlies 30+ kt aloft
IMC / Low Ceilings CONDITIONAL Departure (KSQL), Cascade crossing Frontal position determines this
Marine Layer at Departure LOCALIZED KSQL departure Standard Bay Area morning concern; burns off by mid-morning typically

Getting Into KBDN

TAF status: Not yet valid.

Bend Muni sits at 3,460 ft MSL on the east (lee) side of the Cascades. This matters a lot. In westerly flow, Bend sits in the lee of the range, which means:

  • Post-frontal: Bend is often in good VFR behind the front. The Cascades wring out the moisture on the windward side. Arrival in post-frontal air is typically a non-event — clear or scattered clouds, possibly gusty surface winds from the west/northwest.
  • Pre-frontal or during frontal passage: Bend can go IFR quickly. Moisture wrapping around or over the Cascades can bring low ceilings, possible snow, and rapidly deteriorating conditions.
  • Wind: Lee-side downslope flow can produce gusty, variable surface winds at KBDN that don't match winds aloft. Cannot confirm runway winds at this range — treat as variable, potentially 15–25 kt with gusts possible.

Terrain note: Do not assume winds aloft translate to runway winds at Bend. Terrain distortion is real here. When you pull the KBDN TAF Sunday evening, pay specific attention to wind direction and gusts. Crosswind component matters on runway 16/34.

Alternates: Redmond (KRDM), 16 nm north of Bend, is the obvious alternate and has instrument approaches. Klamath Falls (KLMT) is a viable secondary alternate to the southeast in better post-frontal air if needed. Both are worth filing as alternates if IFR conditions are possible at KBDN.

Estimated block time: Approximately 2h 15–2h 30m at 12,000 ft depending on winds. Arrival ETA around 16:20Z local.

Fly or No

MARGINAL GO — Provisional. Decision deferred to Sunday evening, March 29.

At 5 days out, the extended prog charts show a Pacific trough system approaching the Pacific Northwest around the time of your flight. This pattern has the potential to put frontal weather, icing, and mountain wave turbulence directly on your route. However, the exact timing is unresolved — if the front clears before your departure, this could easily become a clean VMC flight. No TAFs, winds aloft, icing charts, or turbulence data exist yet for this flight window. You simply cannot make a quality go/no-go today.

Plan to fly. Check everything Sunday night. Be willing to delay by 24 hours if the front hasn't cleared.

What changes this call

  • KBDN TAF shows ceilings below 2,000 ft OVC or winds gusting above 25 kt on arrival — reassess and consider a 24-hour delay or divert plan to KRDM
  • FIP icing charts Sunday show moderate or greater icing probability (>25%) between 8,000–13,000 ft over the Cascades — rethink cruise altitude or delay until post-frontal clearing
  • Any SLD icing signal on FIP over the Cascade crossing — hard no-go for non-FIKI aircraft
  • G-AIRMET or SIGMET for mountain wave turbulence (moderate or greater) active over the Oregon Cascades at planned departure time — delay or reroute east of the highest terrain

If You Go — Do This

  1. Sunday night: Pull KSQL and KBDN TAFs the moment they cover your window. Read the SLC and PDX AFDs. Check FIP icing charts at 9,000, 12,000, and 15,000 ft for the Cascade corridor. Check G-AIRMETs. This is when the real decision happens.
  2. File IFR. Not because you expect IMC necessarily, but because this route crosses the Cascades with terrain, potential icing, and mountain wave exposure. IFR gives you structure and ATC assistance if conditions are marginal. Do not fly this as a VFR flight into uncertain Cascade conditions.
  3. Altitude: If icing charts are clean and winds aloft are under 30 kt, 12,000 ft is workable. If winds aloft show 35+ kt over the Cascades, consider requesting 14,000–15,000 ft to get above the worst wave zone — if your aircraft and certification allow. Do not fly at 10,000–12,000 ft into a known mountain wave environment without good reason.
  4. Timing: A 14:00Z departure means you're crossing the Cascades around 15:00–15:30Z. That's still relatively early morning local — before any daytime heating adds convective instability to the mix. This timing is reasonable. Do not delay into afternoon if there's any convective risk.
  5. Alternates: File KRDM as your alternate. Fuel is not a major concern on this leg but carry reserves appropriate for a potential hold or divert.
  6. En route: Get a weather update from Oakland Center or Seattle Center during the flight. If you're picking up any ice over the Cascades that you can't shake with a 2,000 ft altitude change, do not continue — descend to the east side toward Bend or divert.

Confidence

Overall Confidence: LOW

This flight is 5 days out. The extended prog charts are the only data available and they paint a plausible but unresolved picture of a Pacific trough affecting the Pacific Northwest around March 30. Forecast maturity at this range is limited — frontal timing can shift by 12–24 hours easily, which is the difference between a clean VMC flight and a genuine IMC/icing no-go.

Key unknowns that will resolve by Sunday evening:

  • Whether the Pacific low and associated front have cleared the route by 14:00Z Monday
  • Actual icing conditions over the Cascades (FIP charts)
  • Winds aloft at 12,000 ft — determines both mountain wave risk and headwind/tailwind component
  • KBDN arrival conditions — the TAF will tell you the story

Do not make a final decision today. Revisit Sunday night with full short-range data. This briefing is a planning tool only.
